coredata.dc:title: Improved charge extraction with N-doped carbon quantum dots in dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yuanyuan
Duan, Jialong
He, Benlin
Jiao, Zhengbo
Tang, Qunwei
coredata.dc:description: Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) have narrow spectral response and serious electron-hole recombination, which are two burdens for photovoltaic performance enhancement. In order to make high-performance DSSCs featured by wide-spectral absorption and fast charge extraction, we present here experimental realization of co-sensitization of nitrogen (N)-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with N719 dye. Arising from up-conversion and hole extraction behaviors of N-CQDs, a maximized power conversion efficiency (PCE) as high as 9.29% under one sun illumination is achieved for N300-CQDs/N719 co-sensitized DSSC in comparison with 8.09% for N300-CQDs-free device. Due to light storing and emitting characteristics of photofluorescent long persistence phosphors in mesoscopic titanium dioxide/long persistence phosphor (m-TiO2/LPP) photoanode, electricity is persistently generated when ceasing sunlight illumination. This work is far from optimization, but the physical proof-of-concept co-sensitization and fast charge extraction may remarkably widen light-response windows and promote electron-hole separation for advanced photovoltaic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dye-sensitized solar cells
Carbon quantum dots
Charge extraction
Wide spectral absorption
Energy conversion devices

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Hydrolysis reaction of sucrose to glucose and fructose. (B) Conversion from citric acid to N-CQDs. (C) Conversion from glucose and fructose to N-CQDs. TEM images of N300-CQDs at (D) low and (E) high resolutions. (F) The size distribution and (G) line-profile analysis of N300-CQDs.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra for (A) C 1s, (B) N 1s of N300-CQDs. (C) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and (D) Raman spectra of N300-CQDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of the diluted N300-CQDs. (B) down and (D) up-converted PL emission spectra of optimized N300-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (C) and (E) show the photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra. (F) PL emission spectra of m-TiO2/LPP photoanode sensitized by N300-CQDs.

(A) Schematic diagram of the N300-CQDs and N719 co-sensitized DSSCs with a m-TiO2/LPP photoanode. (B) Energy level distribution and charge transfer processes at photoanode/electrolyte interface. Representative photo J-V characteristics of the solar cells sensitized by (C) various N-CQDs and (D) N719 dye and N300-CQDs/N719 under simulation solar irradiation (100 mW cm−2, AM 1.5), and (E) in completely dark conditions. (F) Dark J-V curves for N300-CQDs sensitized solar cells using m-TiO2/LPP photoanode at various decay times.
The FTIR bonds and positions of N300-CQDs.
Light and dark photovoltaic parameters for corresponding DSSCs.

head.ce:title: Improved charge extraction with N-doped carbon quantum dots in dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of New Energy Technology, College of Information Science and Technology, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) have narrow spectral response and serious electron-hole recombination, which are two burdens for photovoltaic performance enhancement. In order to make high-performance DSSCs featured by wide-spectral absorption and fast charge extraction, we present here experimental realization of co-sensitization of nitrogen (N)-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with N719 dye. Arising from up-conversion and hole extraction behaviors of N-CQDs, a maximized power conversion efficiency (PCE) as high as 9.29% under one sun illumination is achieved for N300-CQDs/N719 co-sensitized DSSC in comparison with 8.09% for N300-CQDs-free device. Due to light storing and emitting characteristics of photofluorescent long persistence phosphors in mesoscopic titanium dioxide/long persistence phosphor (m-TiO2/LPP) photoanode, electricity is persistently generated when ceasing sunlight illumination. This work is far from optimization, but the physical proof-of-concept co-sensitization and fast charge extraction may remarkably widen light-response windows and promote electron-hole separation for advanced photovoltaic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy harvest by advanced energy conversion devices has been a persistent objective in modern nanoscience and nanotechnology [1–3]. Among various energy devices, photovoltaics such as dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) having mesoscopic structures are regarded as promising alternatives because they are featured with environmental-friendliness, cost-effectiveness and zero-emissions [4–8]. Until now, narrow spectral response and high electron-hole recombination rate have retarded the further development of these photovoltaic devices. Various materials and technologies have been applied to harvest more solar energy, and therefore realizing more power output. Inorganic quantum dots (QD) such as PbS QDs [9] and GaSb QDs [10] are applied to absorb near-infrared (NIR) light. However, they are environment hazardous and their toxicity has been an environmental burden for widespread applications. Due to zero toxicity [11], size-dependent optical absorption [12] and multiple exciton generation effects [13], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are ideal light absorbers in mesoscopic solar cells [14–16] and also widely used in other fields, such as lithium-ion batteries, and sensors [17]. In fact, the DSSCs with CQD-only sensitizers yield lower power conversion efficiency (PCE) because of weak affinity between CQDs and mesoscopic titanium dioxide (m-TiO2) [18]. Moreover, state-of-the-art N719 dye has relatively narrow spectral absorption and serious electron-hole recombination, leading to unsatisfactory PCE in final DSSCs. A solution to this impasse is to make wide-spectral absorption and fast charge extraction solar cells for energy harvest and conversion. Another challenging problem for photovoltaic devices is that they can only realize electricity generation under sunlight illumination, and the power generation is low or zero in dark-light conditions. Therefore, a challenging but promising object is to realize persistent electricity generation in multi-weather conditions. Recently, we have creatively made bifunctional DSSCs tailored by graphene [19], graphene based coating films [20] and platinum alloys [21] to harvest solar and rain energies. A remaining problem is that the above-mentioned solar cells becomes unusable at rain-free nights and other dark environments.

In the current work, N-CQDs are used to widen absorption spectra and promote charge extraction by co-sensitizing with N719 dye. The experimental results have shown that the PCE is markedly enhanced when using co-sensitizers from N300-CQDs and N719 dye in comparison with N719-only based DSSC. The mechanism behind this enhancement is that N300-CQDs not only widen light-absorbing window from visible to NIR region, but also promote electron-hole separation and charge extraction.

N-CQDs were prepared through a bottom-up method according to the following procedures. In details, 0.5 g of 200-mesh strawberry powders and stoichiometric NH3·H2O (50 μL of NH3·H2O for N50-CQDs, 100 μL of NH3·H2O for N100-CQDs, 300 μL of NH3·H2O for N300-CQDs, 500 μL of NH3·H2O for N500-CQDs) were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water. After vigorous agitation for 10 min, the reagent solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 170 °C for 3 h to obtain N-CQDs. Subsequently, the N-CQDs were repeatedly dialyzed by a 3500D membrane for 24 h to obtain homogeneous N-CQDs solution and then stored at 5 °C. The homogeneous N-CQDs solution can be used to make N-CQDs sensitized TiO2 photoanodes or be dried at 60 °C to obtain N-CQDs powders.

TiO2 colloid was synthesized according to the detailed procedures reported previously [22]. Colloidal TiO2 films with a thickness of around 10 μm were prepared by coating TiO2 colloid onto clean fluorine-doped SnO2 (FTO) glass substrates (12 Ω square−1) by a doctor-blade method. Subsequently, the colloidal TiO2 films were calcined in a muffle furnace at 450 °C for 30 min in air. The m-TiO2 electrodes with an active area of 0.5 × 0.5 cm2 were immersed in a 0.5 mM N719 ethanol solution for 24 h, then N719TiO2 photoanodes were sensitized by N-CQDs solution for 20 h to obtain N-CQDs and N719 co-sensitized m-TiO2 photoanodes. Finally, ultrafine green-emitting LPPs powders (H1B, purchased from Shenzhen HuiDuoSheng Luminous Material Co., Ltd) were dispersed in anhydrous acetonitrile to make a homogenous solution with a concentration of 0.25 g mL−1, which was spin-coated onto sensitized m-TiO2 electrodes to obtain photofluorescent photoanodes.

The pyrolyzed platinum (Pt) films deposited on FTO glass substrates were used as counter electrodes (CEs). Every DSSC device was built by sandwiching an I−/I3 − redox electrolyte between a Pt CE and a sensitized m-TiO2/LPP (or m-TiO2) photoanode and sealed with a Surlyn film. The liquid redox electrolyte including 0.1 M tetraethylammonium iodide, 0.1 M tetramethylammonium iodide, 0.1 M NaI, 0.1 M KI, 0.1 M LiI, 0.05 M I2 and 0.05 M 4-tert-butyl-pyridine in acetonitrile was injected into interspace to obtain N-CQDs and N719 co-sensitized DSSCs. N719-only or N-CQDs-only solar cells were also made by similar procedures.

The current density-voltage (J-V) curves of solar cells were recorded on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation under irradiation of simulated solar light with the light intensity controlled at 100 mW cm−2 xenon arc lamp. In order to avoid stray light, a black mask was applied during the testing process. To eliminate experimental impacts, the corresponding photovoltaic test was repeated at least 15 times to control the experimental errors within ±5%. The solar cells were illuminated for 1 min before testing dark J-V curves. The fluorescent light intensity of m-TiO2/LPP photoanode (P in) was determined by a standard silicon solar cell, then the PCE under dark conditions (PCEdark) of as-prepared solar cells can be calculated according to PCEdark = P max/P in.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) characterization was carried out to estimate LUMO and HOMO energy levels of N300-CQDs by using a standard three-electrode system. The supporting electrolyte was acetonitrile containing 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6). The CV curve was scanned from −1.4 to +1.5 V with a scanning rate of 50 mV s−1. The LUMO and HOMO levels are calculated according to E LUMO = - (E red + 4.4) eV, E HOMO = - (E ox + 4.4) eV.

N-CQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method at 170 °C for 3 h. The main components of strawberry powders include sucrose and critic acid, while a sucrose molecule will split into a glucose molecule and a fructose molecule during the hydrothermal process (Fig. 1 A). Glucose, fructose and citric acid can be converted into N-CQDs through dehydration reactions at high temperatures (Fig. 1B and C) [23,24]. The as-synthesized N-CQDs solution is yellowish brown, and the emission color is blue photoluminescence under excitation with UV illumination. As shown in Fig. S21, N100-CQDs solution has the brightest excitation in visual, however, the N300-CQD presents the highest photofluorescence intensity measured by a standard silicon solar cell. Therefore, N300-CQDs are used to co-sensitize with N719 dye for better photovoltaic performances.

The corresponding N300-CQDs are characterized by TEM observation, as shown in Fig. 1D, achieving a good dispersion and a circular shape. Additionally, the HRTEM image in Fig. 1E indicates an interplanar d-spacing increases from 0.226 nm for pristine CQDs [25] to 0.241 nm for N300-CQDs (Fig. 1G), cross-checking the successful entering of guest N into host CQDs lattice [25,26]. The N300-CQDs shows a uniform size distribution with an average size of 3.01 nm, which can be fitted by a Gaussian curve (Fig. 1F). 58% of the N300-CQDs have a narrow size distribution ranging from 2.7 to 3.3 nm, this indicates that the hydrothermal method contributes to the formation of mono-dispersed N-CQDs.

Due to the imperfection or destruction of sp2 hybridization, a D band is measured at 1345 cm−1, relating to disordered induce-scattering (Fig. 2D). The detection of the D band pertains to the conjugated structure destruction by oxygenic groups and the small sp2 cluster size. The G band determined at 1571 cm−1 is consistent with the E 2g mode of graphite and vibration of sp2 bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice. By comparing I D/I G (0.953) of the N300-CQDs, we can draw a conclusion that the hydrothermal method is efficient for N300-CQDs synthesis.

A strong ultraviolet absorption band centered at 270 nm is determined for the diluted N50-CQDs, N100-CQDs, N300-CQDs and N500-CQDs solutions (Fig. 3 A and Fig. S2), which related to n → π∗ transition of C = O bonds [29]. The linear dependence between the maximal absorbance and 1/dilution time for the peak at 270 nm illustrates that the distribution of N-CQDs does not depend on concentration. Fig. 3B show the down-converted PL emission spectra of N300-CQDs solutions by applying various excitation wavelengths (λ ex). The maximal emission wavelengths (λ em) and PL intensity strongly depend on λ ex. A broad emission peak centered at λ em = 466 nm is measured at λ ex = 370 nm. As λ ex increasing from 370 to 470 nm, the PL intensity increases gradually and the emission peak position has a red shift until the maximized peak intensity occurs at λ ex = 470 nm. When further increase to λ ex > 470 nm, the PL intensity gradually reduces. The dependence of λ em on λ ex contributes to excitation energy traps of oxygenic (COOH, OH, COC, etc.) groups. Similarly, the up-converted PL emission peaks are shifted from 457 to 550 nm by tuning excitation wavelength from 700 to 1000 nm, as shown in Fig. 3D. The phenomenon of emission wavelength and intensity dependent on excitation wavelength has also observed in other carbon-based QDs [30,31]. The up-converted ability of the N300-CQDs should be attributed to an anti-Stokes transition [30] or multiphoton active process [31,32], in which the simultaneous absorption of two or more photons leads to the emission of light at a short wavelength than that of the excitation. The emission peaks are located at 525 nm and 536 nm when subjected to excitation at 470 nm and 950 nm (Fig. 3C and E), which are consistent with PL emission spectra in Fig. 3B and D. The N300-CQDs sensitized m-TiO2/LPP photoanode is subjected to PL characterization (Fig. 3F), emblematical emission peaks at λ ex = 510 nm are determined by tuning λ ex from 450 to 950 nm, which are mainly in virtue of the PL emission of LPP materials. The released fluorescence is re-absorbed by m-TiO2 supported N300-CQDs. In this fashion, the LPPs can markedly broaden the absorption spectrum to NIR light.

Due to the photo-excitation characteristics of N-CQDs, they are applied in DSSCs as light-harvesting materials. When exposed to standard sunlight, the N-CQDs absorb incident light to excite electrons. These photogenerated electrons flow to the conduction band of TiO2 and then jump to FTO layer along percolating m-TiO2 pathways. Notably, the photovoltaic performances are dependent on the dosage of NH3·H2O and therefore N doping contents. The J sc, V oc, FF and PCE values are 0.226 mA cm−2, 0.380 V, 44% and 0.0378% for N50-CQDs tailored solar cell (Fig. 4 C). All of these data are increased by increasing NH3·H2O dosage to 300 μL, as summarized in Table 2 , the corresponding solar cell yields a maximized J sc of 0.581 mA cm−2 and a V oc of 0.515 V. However, these parameters decline when a larger dosage of NH3·H2O is used to prepare N-CQDs. The possible mechanism behind this is the destruction of conjugated structure in carbon plane.

In order to increase electricity generation, N300-CQDs is co-sensitized with N719 dye. The co-sensitized solar cell devices are made according to the schematic diagram in Fig. 4A, and the corresponding relative energy level diagram of different layers is displayed in Fig. 4B. By performing cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement [33], as shown Fig. S14, the energy levels of HOMO and LUMO of N300-CQDs are determined to be −5.28 and −3.12 eV below vacuum lever, respectively. The matching HOMO value (−5.28 eV) of N300-CQDs between HOMO value (−5.45 eV) of dye and redox potential of I−/I3 − couples (−4.9 eV) ensures a promoting hole injection from N719 (−5.45 eV) to N300-CQDs (−5.28 eV) then to electrolyte (−4.9 eV). At the same time, the matching LUMO value (−3.12 eV) of N300-CQDs to LUMO value (−3.85 eV) of dye then to TiO2 (−4.2 eV) and FTO layer (−4.5 eV) demonstrates that the photo-induced electrons can swimmingly flow from N300-CQDs (−3.12 eV) to N719 dye (−3.85 eV), TiO2 (−4.2 eV) and finally to FTO layer (−4.5 eV). As a result, the realization of better energy level alignment contributes to fast electron-hole separation and extraction. Besides, N300-CQDs can absorb near-infrared light below 1100 nm and convert it to visible light for re-absorption by N719 dye, which ensure more electron excitation. Furthermore, N300-CQDs can somewhat act as barrier between oxidized species (I3 −) in electrolyte and photogenerated electrons at photoanode/electrolyte interface to restrain recombination reaction. As shown in Fig. 4D, the standard N719TiO2/electrolyte/Pt CE solar cell achieves a photoelectric conversion efficiency of 8.09% (Voc = 0.721 V, J sc = 17.3 mA cm−2, FF = 64.9%), and it increases to 9.29% (V oc = 0.736 V, J sc = 18.6 mA cm−2, FF = 67.9%) through co-sensitized with N300-CQDs. The experimental results cross-check the better energy level alignment.

In order to realize electricity generation in darkness, LPP materials are incorporated into photoanodes due to the storage of solar energy toward unabsorbed incident light and subsequent green emission (Fig. 4E). The maximized efficiency is up to 26.5% (V oc = 0.423 V, J sc = 0.0754 mA cm−2, FF = 49.9%) according to PCEdark = P max/P in, as summarized in Table 2. This is a representative data from over 15 devices constructed at the same processes. However, the electricity generation is not stable and declining quickly within the first 3600 s due to rapid energy release from LPP (Fig. 4F and Table S1).

In summary, N-CQDs are successfully converted by a bottom-up method using strawberry powders carbon sources and co-sensitized with commercial N719 dye to form high-performance DSSCs. Using the up-conversion and fast charge extraction abilities of optimized N300-CQDs as well as photofluorescent behaviors of LPP, the solar cell yields a PCE of 9.29% under sunlight and a PCE of 26.5% in darkness. When ceasing sunlight illumination, the power generation in the dark can last for hours. This finding confirm the functions N300-CQDs in wide-spectral absorption and promoted charge extraction.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21503202, 61774139) and Director Foundation from Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology (QNLM201702).
